summary: Gruber reviews Apple's first M1 Macs and declares them an astonishing leap forward — the most exciting Macs technically since 1994's PowerPC transition. Unlike previous architecture shifts made from desperation, this one comes from overwhelming strength: the M1 delivers dramatically better performance, silent operation, and all-day battery life while looking identical to its Intel predecessors. He argues that the M1's greatest advantages are qualitative rather than quantitative — silence, coolness, and responsiveness that don't show up in benchmarks. He explains how Apple's vertically integrated hardware-software design, particularly around reference counting and memory management, gives M1 Macs structural advantages that general-purpose architectures can never match. While praising Rosetta's seamless Intel emulation and improved FaceTime camera processing, he sharply criticizes the iOS-apps-on-Mac feature as a rare un-Apple-like compromise in quality.
key_points: The M1 Macs' most important advantages are qualitative — silent operation, cool temperatures, instant wake — rather than benchmarkable numbers, representing Apple's emphasis on 'niceness' as a design value.

Apple's software architecture (reference counting via retain/release) is uniquely optimized on Apple Silicon, making NSObject operations 5x faster than Intel and explaining why M1 Macs perform well with less RAM.
This is the first Mac architecture transition made from strength rather than desperation — Intel's x86 dominance is definitively over in the laptop space.
Rosetta 2 is a technical tour de force: Intel apps running in translation on M1 outperform actual Intel CPUs in MacBook Airs and most MacBook Pros.
iOS apps running on macOS are a rare quality miss for Apple — ranging from terrible to merely OK, feeling foreign and un-Apple-like in their 'better than nothing, ship it' philosophy.
money_quotes: The logic behind Apple's transition now to Apple Silicon is this: If you can beat them handily, do it.
What you need to understand is that the best aspects of these Macs aren't benchmarkable. It's about how nice they are.
The defining characteristic of a Macintosh computer is that it's nice.

It's possible to make good computers with general purpose hardware and general purpose software created by completely separate teams. But such systems will never be as good as hardware and software designed hand-in-hand together.

Rosetta performs great and compatibility is seemingly seamless. This is a technical tour de force, a home run for Apple that will mostly go unheralded by typical users because the entire point is that they shouldn't notice or care.
You can add as many muches as you want, and it won't convey the seismic shift the M1 represents.
Steve Jobs would have fucking loved these M1 Macs.
key_insight: The M1 Macs prove that vertically integrated hardware-software design produces qualitative advantages — silence, coolness, responsiveness — that no general-purpose architecture can match, and these unquantifiable qualities matter more than benchmarks.
